title: Next greater element
description: Find next greater element for each array element

examples.explanation:
Array: [4, 5, 2, 25]

For i=0 (value=4):
  j=1: 5 > 4 → found next greater = 5, break
  result[0] = 5

For i=1 (value=5):
  j=2: 2 ≤ 5 → continue
  j=3: 25 > 5 → found next greater = 25, break
  result[1] = 25

For i=2 (value=2):
  j=3: 25 > 2 → found next greater = 25, break
  result[2] = 25

For i=3 (value=25):
  No elements to the right → result[3] = -1

Final result: [5, 25, 25, -1]

Array: [13, 7, 6, 12]

For i=0 (value=13):
  j=1: 7 ≤ 13 → continue
  j=2: 6 ≤ 13 → continue
  j=3: 12 ≤ 13 → continue
  No greater element found → result[0] = -1

For i=1 (value=7):
  j=2: 6 ≤ 7 → continue
  j=3: 12 > 7 → found next greater = 12, break
  result[1] = 12

For i=2 (value=6):
  j=3: 12 > 6 → found next greater = 12, break
  result[2] = 12

For i=3 (value=12):
  No elements to the right → result[3] = -1

Final result: [-1, 12, 12, -1]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, scan all subsequent elements to find the first greater one. Use nested loops, making it straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Next Greater Element
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to find next greater element for each array element.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach solves the next greater element problem by directly implementing the definition: for each element in the array, we need to find the first element to its right that is greater than it. This is achieved through nested loops where the outer loop iterates through each element, and the inner loop scans all subsequent elements to find the first greater one. The intuition is straightforward: we're essentially performing a linear search for each element, checking every possible candidate that comes after it. This guarantees correctness because we examine all possibilities, but it's inefficient for large arrays due to the quadratic time complexity.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an empty result array of the same length as input
2. For each element at index i from 0 to n-1:
   a. Set next_greater = -1 (default value if none found)
   b. For each element at index j from i+1 to n-1:
      i. If arr[j] > arr[i], set next_greater = arr[j] and break
   c. Store next_greater in result[i]
3. Return result array

Time complexity: O(n²) because for each of n elements, we potentially scan up to n-1 subsequent elements. In worst case (descending array), we scan all remaining elements for each element.
Space complexity: O(1) additional space (excluding output array), as we only use a few variables.

Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the key subroutine of scanning subsequent elements, which is isolated in L1.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to break after finding the first greater element (would find the last greater instead of first)
2. Not handling the case where no greater element exists (should return -1)
3. Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries
4. Confusing array indices with array values

Real code pattern:
def nextGreaterElement(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    result = [-1] * n
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            if arr[j] > arr[i]:
                result[i] = arr[j]
                break
    return result

examples.trace:
Array: [4, 5, 2, 25]

For i=0 (value=4):
  j=1: 5 > 4 → found next greater = 5, break
  result[0] = 5

For i=1 (value=5):
  j=2: 2 ≤ 5 → continue
  j=3: 25 > 5 → found next greater = 25, break
  result[1] = 25

For i=2 (value=2):
  j=3: 25 > 2 → found next greater = 25, break
  result[2] = 25

For i=3 (value=25):
  No elements to the right → result[3] = -1

Final result: [5, 25, 25, -1]

Array: [13, 7, 6, 12]

For i=0 (value=13):
  j=1: 7 ≤ 13 → continue
  j=2: 6 ≤ 13 → continue
  j=3: 12 ≤ 13 → continue
  No greater element found → result[0] = -1

For i=1 (value=7):
  j=2: 6 ≤ 7 → continue
  j=3: 12 > 7 → found next greater = 12, break
  result[1] = 12

For i=2 (value=6):
  j=3: 12 > 6 → found next greater = 12, break
  result[2] = 12

For i=3 (value=12):
  No elements to the right → result[3] = -1

Final result: [-1, 12, 12, -1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Search for First Greater Element
ladders.desc: Find the first element greater than a given value in a subarray.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. Given a starting index and value, we need to scan the remaining array to find the first element that exceeds the given value. The intuition is simple: we're looking for the nearest larger element to the right, so we scan linearly until we find one that satisfies the condition. This is essentially a modified linear search where we're not looking for equality but for a greater value, and we stop at the first match.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: array arr, start index i, value target
2. For index j from i+1 to end of array:
   a. If arr[j] > target, return arr[j] (first greater found)
3. If loop completes without finding, return -1 (no greater element)

Time complexity: O(n) in worst case where we scan the entire remaining array.
Space complexity: O(1) as we only use a few variables.

Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called for each element in the full problem, making the overall solution O(n²).

Common mistakes:
1. Starting search from wrong index (should be i+1, not i)
2. Not returning immediately when found (would continue searching)
3. Comparing with wrong value (should compare with target, not arr[i] if i changes)
4. Forgetting to return -1 when no element found

Real code pattern:
def findFirstGreater(arr, start_idx, target):
    for j in range(start_idx + 1, len(arr)):
        if arr[j] > target:
            return arr[j]
    return -1

examples.trace:
Array: [4, 5, 2, 25], start at index 0+1=1, target=4

j=1: arr[1]=5, 5 > 4 → found! Return 5
Search stops immediately at first greater element found.

Array: [13, 7, 6, 12], start at index 0+1=1, target=13

j=1: arr[1]=7, 7 ≤ 13 → continue
j=2: arr[2]=6, 6 ≤ 13 → continue
j=3: arr[3]=12, 12 ≤ 13 → continue
End of array reached → no element > 13 found → return -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing Elements in Nested Loops
ladders.desc: The pattern of comparing each element with all subsequent elements using nested loops.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic represents the fundamental algorithmic pattern of the brute force approach: using nested loops to compare each element with every element that comes after it. The intuition is that to find relationships between elements based on their positions, we need to examine all possible pairs where the second element comes after the first. This exhaustive comparison guarantees we don't miss any potential matches. The outer loop represents the 'current element' we're evaluating, while the inner loop represents all 'future candidates' that could be its next greater element.

Step-by-step pattern:
1. Outer loop: for i in range(0, n):
   This selects each element as the reference point
2. Inner loop: for j in range(i+1, n):
   This examines all elements to the right of position i
3. Comparison: if arr[j] > arr[i]:
   Checks if the candidate is greater than current element
4. Action: Take appropriate action (break, store result, etc.)

Time complexity: The nested loops create O(n²) comparisons in worst case.
Space complexity: O(1) for the comparison logic itself.

Connection to L1: This comparison pattern is what enables the linear search sub-routine to work for each element.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting inner loop at wrong index (should be i+1, not 0 or i)
2. Not breaking when condition met (would continue unnecessary comparisons)
3. Comparing arr[i] with arr[i] (self-comparison) if starting at i instead of i+1
4. Modifying array during comparison which affects subsequent comparisons

Real code pattern:
for i in range(len(arr)):
    current = arr[i]
    for j in range(i+1, len(arr)):
        candidate = arr[j]
        if candidate > current:
            # Found what we're looking for
            break

examples.trace:
Outer i=0 (value=4):
  Inner j=1: compare 5 > 4? YES → found
  (j=2,3 not reached due to break)

Outer i=1 (value=5):
  Inner j=2: compare 2 > 5? NO → continue
  Inner j=3: compare 25 > 5? YES → found

Outer i=2 (value=2):
  Inner j=3: compare 25 > 2? YES → found

Outer i=3 (value=25):
  No inner loop (j starts at 4, beyond array)

Total comparisons made: 3 (not 6) due to early breaks

Outer i=0 (value=3):
  Inner j=1: compare 1 > 3? NO → continue
  Inner j=2: compare 4 > 3? YES → found

Outer i=1 (value=1):
  Inner j=2: compare 4 > 1? YES → found

Outer i=2 (value=4):
  Inner j=3: compare 2 > 4? NO → continue
  End of inner loop → not found

Outer i=3 (value=2):
  No inner loop

Total comparisons: 4

testCases.expected: Comparisons: i0:j1✗j2✗j3✗j4✗j5✗, i1:j2✗j3✗j4✗j5✗, i2:j3✗j4✗j5✗, i3:j4✗j5✗, i4:j5✗

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Iterating through arrays while tracking and using indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on the fundamental skill of traversing arrays while properly managing indices. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and to compare elements based on their positions, we need to track where we are (current index) and where we're going (future indices). This involves understanding that array indices are offsets from the start, and that we can use these indices to access elements, compare positions, and control loop boundaries. Mastering index tracking is essential for any algorithm that needs to examine relationships between elements at different positions.

Key concepts:
1. Zero-based indexing: First element at index 0, last at n-1
2. Loop variables as indices: Using i, j, etc. to represent positions
3. Index arithmetic: Calculating i+1, n-1, etc.
4. Boundary conditions: Ensuring indices stay within 0 to n-1
5. Relative positioning: Understanding that j > i means element at j comes after element at i

Step-by-step approach:
1. Determine array length n
2. Use for loop with index variable: for i in range(n):
3. Access element using index: current = arr[i]
4. For nested traversal, inner loop starts at i+1: for j in range(i+1, n):
5. Access comparison element: candidate = arr[j]

Time complexity: O(n) for single traversal, O(n²) for nested.
Space complexity: O(1) for index variables.

Connection to L2: Proper index tracking enables the nested loop comparison pattern.

Common mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors (using <= n instead of < n)
2. Confusing index with value (using i instead of arr[i])
3. Modifying loop variable inside loop causing unexpected behavior
4. Not resetting index variables when reusing them

Real code pattern:
n = len(arr)
for i in range(n):          # Outer index
    current = arr[i]
    for j in range(i+1, n): # Inner index, starts after i
        candidate = arr[j]
        # Now we have two elements and their positions

examples.trace:
Array length: 3
Indices: 0, 1, 2

Index 0 → value 10
Index 1 → value 20
Index 2 → value 30

When i=0, j ranges from 1 to 2:
  Compare arr[0]=10 with arr[1]=20 (indices 0 and 1)
  Compare arr[0]=10 with arr[2]=30 (indices 0 and 2)

When i=1, j ranges from 2 to 2:
  Compare arr[1]=20 with arr[2]=30 (indices 1 and 2)

When i=2, j range is empty (i+1=3 ≥ n=3)

Array length: 3
Indices: 0, 1, 2

Index 0 → value 5
Index 1 → value 1
Index 2 → value 4

Traversal with i tracking:
i=0: current=arr[0]=5, j from 1 to 2
  j=1: candidate=arr[1]=1
  j=2: candidate=arr[2]=4

i=1: current=arr[1]=1, j from 2 to 2
  j=2: candidate=arr[2]=4

i=2: current=arr[2]=4, j from 3 to 2 (empty range)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison in Arrays
ladders.desc: Comparing two array elements using relational operators.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation focuses on the fundamental action of comparing two elements from an array. The intuition is simple: to determine relationships between elements (like which is greater), we need to compare their values. In programming, this is done using relational operators (>, <, ≥, ≤, ==, ≠). For the next greater element problem, we specifically use the greater-than (>) operator to check if one element exceeds another. This operation is atomic but crucial—it's the decision point that determines whether we've found what we're looking for.

Key aspects:
1. Accessing elements: Using array[index] syntax to retrieve values
2. Comparison operator: > checks if left operand is greater than right
3. Result: Boolean (true/false) indicating relationship
4. Usage: Typically in conditional statements (if conditions)

Step-by-step operation:
1. Retrieve first element: value1 = arr[i]
2. Retrieve second element: value2 = arr[j]
3. Apply comparison: value2 > value1
4. Use result: if true, second element is greater than first

Time complexity: O(1) constant time operation.
Space complexity: O(1) uses only the values being compared.

Connection to L3: Comparison operations are applied during array traversal when we have two elements to compare.

Common mistakes:
1. Comparing indices instead of values (i > j instead of arr[i] > arr[j])
2. Using wrong comparison operator (≥ instead of > for 'strictly greater')
3. Not considering data types (comparing strings vs numbers)
4. Modifying values during comparison affecting result

Real code pattern:
# Retrieve two elements
first = arr[i]
second = arr[j]

# Compare them
if second > first:
    # second is greater than first
    # Take appropriate action
else:
    # second is not greater than first
    # Continue searching

examples.trace:
Step 1: Retrieve arr[0] = 4
Step 2: Retrieve arr[1] = 5
Step 3: Compare: 5 > 4
Step 4: Evaluate: 5 > 4 is true
Result: true (5 is greater than 4)

Step 1: Retrieve arr[0] = 13
Step 2: Retrieve arr[1] = 7
Step 3: Compare: 7 > 13
Step 4: Evaluate: 7 > 13 is false
Result: false (7 is not greater than 13)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Access and Loops
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of arrays and iterative traversal using loops.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation covers the absolute prerequisites: understanding what arrays are and how to traverse them using loops. An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices. The intuition is that arrays let us organize related data so we can process it systematically. Loops provide the mechanism to visit each element one by one—this is called traversal or iteration. Without these basics, we cannot even begin to solve array problems.

Key concepts:
1. Array definition: Ordered collection of elements, each with an index
2. Zero-based indexing: First element at position 0, not 1
3. Array access: Using array[index] to get or set element at position index
4. Loop structures: for and while loops to repeat actions
5. Loop variable: Typically i, j, etc. that changes each iteration
6. Range function: range(start, stop, step) generates sequence of numbers

Basic array traversal pattern:
1. Get array length: n = len(arr)
2. Set up loop: for i in range(n):
3. Access element: element = arr[i]
4. Process element: Do something with it

Time complexity: O(n) to visit n elements once.
Space complexity: O(1) for the traversal itself.

Connection to L4: Once we can traverse arrays, we can access elements to compare them.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Thinking arrays start at index 1 (they start at 0)
2. Accessing beyond array bounds (index ≥ length)
3. Forgetting to increment loop variable in while loops
4. Modifying array while iterating causing skipped elements
5. Confusing element value with index position

Real code pattern:
# Example array
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]

# Basic traversal
for i in range(len(arr)):
    current_element = arr[i]
    print(f"Index {i} has value {current_element}")

# This would output:
# Index 0 has value 10
# Index 1 has value 20
# Index 2 has value 30
# Index 3 has value 40

examples.trace:
Array: [5, 10, 15]
Length: 3

Loop execution:
Iteration 1: i=0 → arr[0]=5 → process 5
Iteration 2: i=1 → arr[1]=10 → process 10
Iteration 3: i=2 → arr[2]=15 → process 15

End of loop (i=3 not less than 3)

Elements visited in order: 5, 10, 15

Array: [100, 200]
Length: 2

Loop execution:
Iteration 1: i=0 → arr[0]=100 → process 100
Iteration 2: i=1 → arr[1]=200 → process 200

End of loop (i=2 not less than 2)

Elements visited in order: 100, 200

approaches.name: Stack with Monotonic Decreasing Order O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to maintain indices of elements in decreasing order. Iterate through the array, popping from the stack when a greater element is found, assigning it as the next greater element for popped indices.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Next Greater Element Using Monotonic Decreasing Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find next greater element for each array element using stack with monotonic decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding, for each element in an array, the first element to its right that is strictly greater. The naive O(n²) approach compares each element with all subsequent elements. The optimal O(n) approach uses a monotonic decreasing stack to track elements awaiting their next greater element. The intuition is that when we encounter a larger element, it becomes the next greater element for all smaller elements in the stack that precede it. We maintain a stack of indices (not values) to preserve order information. As we iterate through the array, we compare the current element with elements at indices stored in the stack. While the stack is not empty and the current element is greater than the element at the top index of the stack, we pop that index and assign the current element as its next greater element. After processing all elements in the stack for the current element, we push the current index onto the stack. This ensures the stack remains in decreasing order of element values. Elements left in the stack after iteration have no next greater element, so we assign -1. The algorithm runs in O(n) time because each element is pushed and popped at most once. Space is O(n) for the stack and result array. This approach efficiently resolves multiple pending comparisons in one pass by leveraging the stack's LIFO property to match elements with their next greater counterparts in reverse order of discovery.

examples.trace:
Initialize result = [-1, -1, -1, -1], stack = [].
Index 0 (value 4): stack empty, push 0 → stack = [0].
Index 1 (value 5): 5 > 4 (value at stack top index 0), pop 0, set result[0] = 5 → result = [5, -1, -1, -1]. Stack empty, push 1 → stack = [1].
Index 2 (value 2): 2 < 5, push 2 → stack = [1, 2].
Index 3 (value 25): 25 > 2 (value at stack top index 2), pop 2, set result[2] = 25 → result = [5, -1, 25, -1]. 25 > 5 (value at stack top index 1), pop 1, set result[1] = 25 → result = [5, 25, 25, -1]. Stack empty, push 3 → stack = [3].
After loop, stack has index 3, set result[3] = -1 → result = [5, 25, 25, -1].

Initialize result = [-1, -1, -1, -1], stack = [].
Index 0 (value 13): stack empty, push 0 → stack = [0].
Index 1 (value 7): 7 < 13, push 1 → stack = [0, 1].
Index 2 (value 6): 6 < 7, push 2 → stack = [0, 1, 2].
Index 3 (value 12): 12 > 6 (value at stack top index 2), pop 2, set result[2] = 12 → result = [-1, -1, 12, -1]. 12 > 7 (value at stack top index 1), pop 1, set result[1] = 12 → result = [-1, 12, 12, -1]. 12 < 13, push 3 → stack = [0, 3].
After loop, stack has indices 0 and 3, set result[0] = -1, result[3] = -1 → result = [-1, 12, 12, -1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Processing One Element Against the Stack
ladders.desc: The core operation of comparing current element with stack elements and updating results.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the dynamic interaction between the current array element and the monotonic decreasing stack. For a given current index i and value arr[i], we repeatedly check if the stack is non-empty and if arr[i] is greater than the element at the top index of the stack. If both conditions hold, we pop the top index j from the stack and set result[j] = arr[i]. This resolves the next greater element for index j. We continue this while loop until either the stack is empty or arr[i] is not greater than the element at the new top index. After exiting the loop, we push i onto the stack. The intuition is that arr[i] serves as a potential next greater element for all smaller elements that came before it and are still waiting in the stack. By processing in this order, we ensure that each element in the stack only gets assigned a next greater element that appears after it in the array. This sub-routine is the engine of the algorithm, executed for each element in the array. Time complexity per element is amortized O(1) because each index is popped at most once. Space complexity involves the stack, which can grow up to O(n). This feeds into the full problem by being the repeated step in the main loop. Common mistakes include comparing values instead of indices, not handling empty stack correctly, or using a while loop incorrectly. Real code pattern: while stack and arr[i] > arr[stack[-1]]: j = stack.pop(); result[j] = arr[i]; stack.append(i).

examples.input: Current index: 2, value: 8, stack: [0, 1] with arr=[5, 6, 8], result=[-1, -1, -1]
examples.trace:
Start with stack = [0, 1], arr[0]=5, arr[1]=6, arr[2]=8, result = [-1, -1, -1].
While stack not empty and 8 > arr[stack[-1]] = arr[1] = 6: pop 1, set result[1] = 8 → result = [-1, 8, -1].
While stack not empty and 8 > arr[stack[-1]] = arr[0] = 5: pop 0, set result[0] = 8 → result = [8, 8, -1].
Stack empty, push 2 → stack = [2].

examples.input: Current index: 3, value: 3, stack: [0, 2] with arr=[4, 1, 2, 3], result=[-1, -1, -1, -1]
examples.trace:
Start with stack = [0, 2], arr[0]=4, arr[2]=2, arr[3]=3, result = [-1, -1, -1, -1].
While stack not empty and 3 > arr[stack[-1]] = arr[2] = 2: pop 2, set result[2] = 3 → result = [-1, -1, 3, -1].
While stack not empty and 3 > arr[stack[-1]] = arr[0] = 4: condition false (3 not > 4), exit loop.
Push 3 → stack = [0, 3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Monotonic Decreasing Stack
ladders.desc: The technique of using a stack to keep elements in decreasing order while processing an array.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves managing a stack such that the elements (via their indices) are stored in strictly decreasing order of their values as we iterate through the array. After processing each element arr[i], we ensure that all indices in the stack correspond to elements with values greater than or equal to arr[i], maintaining the decreasing property. The key insight is that if we encounter an element arr[i] that is greater than the element at the top of the stack, it violates the decreasing order, so we must pop from the stack until order is restored, and in doing so, we assign arr[i] as the next greater element for those popped indices. This logic efficiently finds next greater elements because the stack naturally groups elements that are waiting for a larger element, and when one appears, it resolves multiple pending queries at once. The stack's LIFO structure ensures we process elements in reverse order of their appearance, which matches the definition of 'next greater' (first larger element to the right). Time complexity is O(n) as each index is pushed and popped once. Space is O(n) for the stack. This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the mechanism to update the stack. Common mistakes include not maintaining strict decreasing order or mishandling equal values. Real code pattern: stack = []; for i in range(n): while stack and arr[i] > arr[stack[-1]]: process pop; stack.append(i).

examples.trace:
Start stack = [].
Index 0 (3): stack empty, push 0 → stack = [0] (decreasing: 3).
Index 1 (1): 1 < 3, push 1 → stack = [0,1] (decreasing: 3,1).
Index 2 (4): 4 > 1 (top), pop 1 → stack = [0]. 4 > 3, pop 0 → stack = []. Push 2 → stack = [2] (decreasing: 4).
Index 3 (2): 2 < 4, push 3 → stack = [2,3] (decreasing: 4,2).

Start stack = [].
Index 0 (5): push 0 → stack = [0] (5).
Index 1 (4): 4 < 5, push 1 → stack = [0,1] (5,4).
Index 2 (3): 3 < 4, push 2 → stack = [0,1,2] (5,4,3).
Index 3 (6): 6 > 3, pop 2 → stack = [0,1]. 6 > 4, pop 1 → stack = [0]. 6 > 5, pop 0 → stack = []. Push 3 → stack = [3] (6).

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Index Management
ladders.desc: Using stack to store indices and perform push/pop operations based on comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental use of a stack data structure to manage indices of array elements. Instead of storing values directly, we store indices because they provide access to both the value (via array lookup) and the position for updating results. The stack supports two key operations: push (adding an index to the top) and pop (removing the top index). We use these operations in conjunction with comparisons between the current array element and the element at the top index of the stack. The intuition is that the stack serves as a waiting list for elements that haven't yet found their next greater element. By pushing indices onto the stack, we defer their resolution until a larger element is encountered. Popping occurs when that larger element is found, allowing us to resolve multiple deferred elements efficiently. This index-based approach is crucial because it maintains the order of elements and enables correct assignment in the result array. Time complexity for push and pop is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack. This feeds into the core logic by providing the basic mechanism to maintain the monotonic stack. Common mistakes include pushing values instead of indices, or not updating the stack correctly after pops. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(i); top_index = stack[-1]; stack.pop().

examples.input: Array: [5, 3, 8], process index 2 with value 8, stack currently [0,1]
examples.trace:
Start stack = [0,1] representing indices of values 5 and 3.
Check top index 1: arr[1] = 3, 8 > 3, so pop index 1 → stack = [0].
Check top index 0: arr[0] = 5, 8 > 5, so pop index 0 → stack = [].
Push current index 2 → stack = [2].

examples.input: Array: [4, 2, 3], process index 2 with value 3, stack currently [0,1]
examples.trace:
Start stack = [0,1] for values 4 and 2.
Check top index 1: arr[1] = 2, 3 > 2, so pop index 1 → stack = [0].
Check top index 0: arr[0] = 4, 3 not > 4, so no pop.
Push current index 2 → stack = [0,2].

testCases.input: Stack: [2,3], top index value from arr=[1,2,3,4]
Stack: [1,4], pop top then push index 6
Stack: [0], compare arr[0]=7 with current val=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of comparing two array elements to determine if one is greater.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing the value of the current array element with the value of an element at a specific index, typically the top of the stack. The comparison checks if the current element is strictly greater than the other element. This is a core predicate in the algorithm: if arr[current_index] > arr[stack_top_index], then the current element is the next greater element for the stack top element. The intuition is that we are looking for the first element to the right that is larger, so this comparison identifies candidates. It's a simple relational operation but is used repeatedly in a loop to resolve multiple pending comparisons. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is negligible. This feeds into the building block by providing the condition for stack pops. Common mistakes include using >= instead of > (which would handle equal values incorrectly for 'strictly greater'), or comparing indices instead of values. Real code pattern: if arr[i] > arr[stack[-1]]: then perform action.

examples.input: Current value: 10, stack top index: 2, arr[2] = 7
examples.trace: We have arr[i] = 10 and arr[stack[-1]] = arr[2] = 7. Perform comparison: 10 > 7 evaluates to true. This means 10 is greater than 7, so we can use 10 as the next greater element for the element at index 2.

examples.input: Current value: 4, stack top index: 1, arr[1] = 6
examples.trace: arr[i] = 4, arr[stack[-1]] = arr[1] = 6. Comparison: 4 > 6 evaluates to false. This means 4 is not greater than 6, so it cannot be the next greater element for the element at index 1. The stack top remains, and we move on.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: The basic concept of accessing elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level covers the fundamental idea of an array as a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index. An index is an integer that represents the position of an element in the array, starting from 0 for the first element. For example, in an array arr = [10, 20, 30], arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, and arr[2] = 30. Accessing an element by its index allows us to retrieve or modify its value in O(1) time. This is crucial for the next greater element problem because we need to compare values at specific positions and update results based on indices. The intuition is that indices provide a way to navigate the array sequentially and refer to elements without moving them. In the algorithm, we use indices to keep track of positions in the stack and to update the result array correctly. Time complexity for array access is constant. Space complexity for the array is O(n) to store n elements. This feeds into the basic operation by enabling value comparisons through index-based lookups. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in math but not in programming) or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: value = arr[index]; result[index] = new_value.

examples.trace: Given array arr = [5, 8, 3]. The index 1 refers to the second element (since indices start at 0). So, arr[1] = 8. We can use this to get the value for comparison or other operations.

examples.input: Array: [2, 4, 6, 8], set result at index 2 to 10
examples.output: Result array becomes [ -1, -1, 10, -1 ] if initialized with -1
examples.trace: Start with result array = [-1, -1, -1, -1]. We want to update the value at index 2 to 10. So, we set result[2] = 10. Now result = [-1, -1, 10, -1]. This shows how indices allow precise updates.

approaches.name: Reverse Iteration with Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the end of the array, using a stack to track potential greater elements. For each element, pop smaller elements from the stack to find the next greater one, then push the current element.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Next Greater Element for Each Array Element
ladders.desc: Complete solution using reverse iteration with stack to find next greater element for each position
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires finding the next greater element for every element in an array. The reverse iteration with stack approach works by processing elements from right to left, maintaining a stack that stores potential candidates for being the next greater element. For each element, we pop from the stack all elements that are smaller than or equal to the current element (since they cannot be the next greater for any element to the left). After popping, if the stack is not empty, the top element is the next greater element for the current position. We then push the current element onto the stack. This approach works because when we process from right to left, the stack maintains elements in decreasing order from top to bottom, representing potential next greater elements for elements we haven't processed yet. The time complexity is O(n) because each element is pushed and popped at most once. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack and result array. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete algorithm that uses the key subroutine of finding the next greater for a single element. Common mistakes include not handling the case when no greater element exists (should return -1), incorrectly popping from stack (should pop while top ≤ current, not just <), and forgetting to push current element after processing. Real code pattern: def nextGreaterElements(arr): n = len(arr); result = [-1]*n; stack = []; for i in range(n-1, -1, -1): while stack and stack[-1] <= arr[i]: stack.pop(); if stack: result[i] = stack[-1]; stack.append(arr[i]); return result

examples.trace:
Start: arr = [4,5,2,25], result = [-1,-1,-1,-1], stack = []
Step 1 (i=3, arr[3]=25): while stack empty → skip; stack empty → result[3] stays -1; push 25 → stack = [25]
Step 2 (i=2, arr[2]=2): while stack and 25 ≤ 2? 25 > 2 → skip; stack not empty → result[2] = 25; push 2 → stack = [25,2]
Step 3 (i=1, arr[1]=5): while stack and 2 ≤ 5? 2 ≤ 5 → pop 2 → stack = [25]; while stack and 25 ≤ 5? 25 > 5 → skip; stack not empty → result[1] = 25; push 5 → stack = [25,5]
Step 4 (i=0, arr[0]=4): while stack and 5 ≤ 4? 5 > 4 → skip; stack not empty → result[0] = 5; push 4 → stack = [25,5,4]
Final result: [5,25,25,-1]

Start: arr = [13,7,6,12], result = [-1,-1,-1,-1], stack = []
Step 1 (i=3, arr[3]=12): while stack empty → skip; stack empty → result[3] stays -1; push 12 → stack = [12]
Step 2 (i=2, arr[2]=6): while stack and 12 ≤ 6? 12 > 6 → skip; stack not empty → result[2] = 12; push 6 → stack = [12,6]
Step 3 (i=1, arr[1]=7): while stack and 6 ≤ 7? 6 ≤ 7 → pop 6 → stack = [12]; while stack and 12 ≤ 7? 12 > 7 → skip; stack not empty → result[1] = 12; push 7 → stack = [12,7]
Step 4 (i=0, arr[0]=13): while stack and 7 ≤ 13? 7 ≤ 13 → pop 7 → stack = [12]; while stack and 12 ≤ 13? 12 ≤ 13 → pop 12 → stack = []; stack empty → result[0] stays -1; push 13 → stack = [13]
Final result: [-1,12,12,-1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Next Greater for Current Element
ladders.desc: Given current element and stack of potential greater elements, find next greater element
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the algorithm. Given a current element and a stack containing potential next greater elements (maintained in decreasing order from top to bottom), we need to find the next greater element for the current position. The process involves: 1) Popping all elements from the stack that are smaller than or equal to the current element (since if they're ≤ current, they cannot be the next greater for current or any element to the left). 2) After popping, if the stack is not empty, the top element is the next greater element for current. 3) If stack becomes empty, there is no greater element to the right. 4) Finally, push the current element onto the stack (since it becomes a candidate for being next greater for elements to the left). The intuition is that by maintaining a decreasing stack, we ensure that for any element, the first element to its right that's greater will be at the top of the stack after removing all smaller elements. Time complexity for this subroutine is amortized O(1) since each element is popped at most once overall. Space is O(n) for the stack. This connects to L0 as the core operation performed for each element in reverse order. Common mistakes include popping only while top < current (should be ≤), not checking stack emptiness before accessing top, and forgetting to push current element. Real code pattern: def findNextGreater(current, stack): while stack and stack[-1] <= current: stack.pop(); if stack: return stack[-1]; else: return -1; stack.append(current)

examples.trace:
Step 1: stack = [25,10,8], top=8, 8 ≤ 2? 8 > 2 → stop popping
Step 2: stack not empty → top=25 is next greater
Step 3: push 2 → stack becomes [25,10,8,2]
Result: 25

Step 1: stack = [12,10,5], top=5, 5 ≤ 15 → pop 5 → stack=[12,10]
Step 2: top=10, 10 ≤ 15 → pop 10 → stack=[12]
Step 3: top=12, 12 ≤ 15 → pop 12 → stack=[]
Step 4: stack empty → no greater element
Step 5: push 15 → stack=[15]
Result: -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Decreasing Stack While Iterating Backwards
ladders.desc: The technique of using a decreasing stack when processing elements from right to left
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that by iterating from the end of the array and maintaining a stack where elements are in strictly decreasing order from top to bottom, we can efficiently find next greater elements. When we process an element from right to left, the stack contains all elements to the right that could potentially be the next greater element for current or future elements. The decreasing property is crucial: if we have a larger element below a smaller one in the stack, the smaller one would be irrelevant since the larger one would always be a better candidate. The algorithm maintains this property by popping elements that are ≤ current before pushing current. This ensures that for any element, after popping all smaller/equal elements, the top of stack (if exists) is the first element to the right that's greater. The intuition: imagine looking for a taller person behind you in a line; you only care about the first taller person, not shorter ones who are blocked. Time complexity O(n) as each element pushed/popped once. Space O(n). This connects to L1 as the framework that uses the find-next-greater subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include not maintaining strict decreasing order (allowing equal elements breaks logic), iterating forward instead of backward, and not understanding why decreasing stack works. Real code pattern: stack = []; for i from n-1 to 0: # maintain decreasing stack while stack and stack[-1] <= arr[i]: stack.pop(); # use stack top as next greater if exists; stack.append(arr[i])

examples.trace:
Start: stack=[]
Process 2 (i=3): stack empty → push 2 → stack=[2]
Process 4 (i=2): while stack and 2≤4 → pop 2 → stack=[]; push 4 → stack=[4]
Process 1 (i=1): while stack and 4≤1? 4>1 → skip; push 1 → stack=[4,1]
Process 3 (i=0): while stack and 1≤3 → pop 1 → stack=[4]; while stack and 4≤3? 4>3 → skip; push 3 → stack=[4,3]
Final stack: [4,3] (decreasing: 4>3)

Start: stack=[]
Process 1: push 1 → [1]
Process 2: while [1] and 1≤2 → pop 1 → []; push 2 → [2]
Process 3: while [2] and 2≤3 → pop 2 → []; push 3 → [3]
Process 4: while [3] and 3≤4 → pop 3 → []; push 4 → [4]
Process 5: while [4] and 4≤5 → pop 4 → []; push 5 → [5]
Final stack: [5] (decreasing trivially)

ladders.title: Building Block — Monotonic Stack Concept
ladders.desc: Understanding stacks that maintain monotonic (increasing/decreasing) order
ladders.explanation: A monotonic stack is a stack where elements are maintained in either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing order. For the next greater element problem, we use a decreasing monotonic stack (elements decrease from top to bottom). The key property is that when we process a new element, we pop elements from the stack until the monotonic property is preserved, then push the new element. This structure is powerful because it allows O(1) access to relevant elements (like the next greater) while ensuring irrelevant elements are removed. The intuition: in a decreasing stack, each element 'dominates' all smaller elements below it for being the next greater candidate. When we encounter a new element, we remove all elements it dominates (those ≤ it) since they're no longer useful. Time complexity for maintaining monotonic stack across n operations is O(n) amortized. Space O(n). This connects to L2 as the specific type of stack used. Common mistakes include not maintaining strict monotonicity (allowing equal elements), confusing increasing vs decreasing stacks, and not understanding why popping preserves correctness. Real code pattern: # Decreasing stack while stack and stack[-1] <= new_element: stack.pop(); stack.append(new_element)

examples.trace:
Start: []
Push 5: [] → [5]
Push 3: 5>3 → [5,3]
Push 8: while [5,3] and 3≤8 → pop 3 → [5]; while [5] and 5≤8 → pop 5 → []; push 8 → [8]
Push 2: 8>2 → [8,2]
Push 6: while [8,2] and 2≤6 → pop 2 → [8]; while [8] and 8≤6? 8>6 → skip; push 6 → [8,6]
Final: [8,6] (decreasing: 8>6)

Start: []
Push 4: [] → [4]
Push 4: while [4] and 4≤4 → pop 4 → []; push 4 → [4]
Push 4: while [4] and 4≤4 → pop 4 → []; push 4 → [4]
Push 4: while [4] and 4≤4 → pop 4 → []; push 4 → [4]
Final: [4] (single element trivially decreasing)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Pop While Condition
ladders.desc: Popping elements from a stack while a condition holds
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves repeatedly removing elements from the top of a stack as long as a certain condition is true. In the context of next greater element, the condition is 'while stack is not empty AND the top element is less than or equal to the current element'. The popping continues until either the stack becomes empty or the top element is greater than the current element. This operation is crucial because it removes elements that are no longer useful as candidates for next greater elements. The intuition: if we have a current element and there's a smaller element in the stack, that smaller element cannot be the next greater for current or any element to the left (since current is larger and would be a better candidate). Time complexity depends on number of pops, but amortized O(1) per operation. Space O(1) extra. This connects to L3 as the mechanism used to maintain monotonic stack property. Common mistakes include not checking stack emptiness before accessing top, using wrong comparison (should be ≤ not < for strict decreasing), and not understanding that popping is destructive but necessary. Real code pattern: while stack and stack[-1] <= current_element: stack.pop()

examples.trace:
Step 1: stack=[8,5,3,1], top=1, condition: stack not empty and 1≤4 → true → pop 1 → stack=[8,5,3]
Step 2: top=3, condition: stack not empty and 3≤4 → true → pop 3 → stack=[8,5]
Step 3: top=5, condition: stack not empty and 5≤4? 5>4 → false → stop
Popped elements: 1,3; remaining: [8,5]

Step 1: stack=[10,7,6], top=6, condition: stack not empty and 6≤12 → true → pop 6 → stack=[10,7]
Step 2: top=7, condition: stack not empty and 7≤12 → true → pop 7 → stack=[10]
Step 3: top=10, condition: stack not empty and 10≤12 → true → pop 10 → stack=[]
Step 4: stack empty → stop
Popped elements: 6,7,10; remaining: []

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Data Structure Basics
ladders.desc: Understanding stack operations: push, pop, peek, and LIFO principle
ladders.explanation: A stack is a fundamental data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. The basic operations are: 1) Push: add an element to the top of the stack. 2) Pop: remove and return the top element from the stack. 3) Peek/Top: view the top element without removing it. 4) IsEmpty: check if stack has no elements. In Python, a list can be used as a stack with append() for push, pop() for pop, and [-1] for peek. The LIFO property means the most recently added element is the first one to be removed. This is intuitive like a stack of plates: you add to the top and remove from the top. Time complexity for all basic operations is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) where n is number of elements stored. This connects to L4 as the underlying structure used for the pop-while operation. Common mistakes include trying to pop from empty stack (should check isEmpty first), confusing stack with queue (FIFO), and not understanding that stack access is limited to top only. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(5) # push; top = stack[-1] # peek; element = stack.pop() # pop; isEmpty = len(stack)==0

examples.input: Operations: push 3, push 5, push 7, pop, peek, push 2
examples.trace:
Start: []
Push 3: [3]
Push 5: [3,5]
Push 7: [3,5,7]
Pop: removes 7 → [3,5]
Peek: returns 5 (stack unchanged) → [3,5]
Push 2: [3,5,2]
Final: [3,5,2]

examples.input: Operations: push 10, pop, push 20, push 30, pop, peek
examples.trace:
Start: []
Push 10: [10]
Pop: removes 10 → []
Push 20: [20]
Push 30: [20,30]
Pop: removes 30 → [20]
Peek: returns 20 → [20]
Final: [20], peek value: 20

testCases.input: Operations: push 2, push 3, push 4, pop, peek
Operations: push 10, push 20, pop, push 30, pop
Operations: push 1, push 1, push 1, pop, pop
Operations: push 6, push 7, push 8, pop, push 9
Operations: push 0, pop, push 1, push 2, pop, peek
